name: Branwen
description: Branwen, daughter of Llŷr and Penarddun, and sister to Bran the Blessed, Manawydan, and Efnisien, is a prominent figure in the Second Branch of the Mabinogi, *Branwen ferch Llŷr*. She is a goddess of love, beauty, and forgiveness, but also of suffering and sacrifice. Her story is one of political marriage, betrayal, and devastating consequences. Branwen's marriage to Matholwch, King of Ireland, was intended to forge peace between the two lands. However, her brother Efnisien's spiteful act of mutilating Matholwch's horses sparked a chain of events leading to war. Branwen endured harsh treatment in Ireland, being forced to work in the kitchens and subjected to physical abuse. She trained a starling to carry a message to her brother Bran, leading to a Welsh invasion of Ireland. Though Branwen was eventually rescued, the war resulted in immense bloodshed and destruction. She returned to Wales, heartbroken and filled with remorse for the devastation her marriage had caused. Branwen died shortly after landing, uttering the poignant words, "I am Branwen, daughter of Llŷr, and from my suffering has sprung this island." Her story serves as a reminder of the destructive power of vengeance and the enduring strength of forgiveness. Branwen's role as a peace weaver gone awry, her resilience in the face of adversity, and her ultimate sacrifice have cemented her place as a tragic yet revered figure in Welsh mythology.
subtitle: Goddess of love, beauty, and sacrifice

keyMyths.title: Branwen's Marriage
keyMyths.description: Branwen's marriage to Matholwch, King of Ireland, was intended to bring peace between Wales and Ireland, but it was marred by Efnisien's actions.

keyMyths.title: Branwen's Imprisonment
keyMyths.description: After being mistreated in Ireland, Branwen was forced into servitude and endured physical abuse, eventually sending a starling to her brother Bran for help.

keyMyths.title: Branwen's Death
keyMyths.description: Returning to Wales after the devastating war, Branwen died of a broken heart, lamenting the destruction that her marriage had caused.

sources.author: Gwyn Jones and Thomas Jones
sources.description: A translation of the Mabinogion tales.
